one_line_takeaway: A diagnostic, benchmark-driven audit showing DeepSeek's models are well-aligned against direct harmful prompts but collapse under jailbreaks (up to 100% ASR), are more exploitable in English than Chinese, and become riskier when reasoning chains are exposed.
key_finding: DeepSeek models show robust behavior on direct harmful queries but brittle safety under jailbreak: on CNSafe_RT, DeepSeek-V3 reaches 95-100% ASR in many categories (e.g., 100% for Ethnic hatred and False information in both languages). Three headline findings: (1) English prompts elicit unsafe content far more than Chinese ones, average ASR gap 21.7% on CNSafe; (2) the reasoning model DeepSeek-R1, which exposes its chain-of-thought, is markedly less safe than V3 (the intro states +30.4% average ASR; the Table-1 analysis states a 31.25% average gap across major categories) — QwQ-32B replicates this reasoning-model pattern (avg ASR 26.6%, highest of the comparison set); (3) jailbreak ASRs climb to as high as 100% in some categories. On T2I, Janus-Pro-7B is riskier than SD-3.5-Large overall (avg ASR 43.7% vs 39.9%), driven by Sexual (74% vs 47%) and Illegal-Activity (61% vs 39%) content, and it will generate fake-news imagery (e.g., 'Trump arrested').
core_contribution: The paper presents what the authors call the first comprehensive safety evaluation of the full DeepSeek model series, spanning LLMs (DeepSeek-R1 671B, DeepSeek-V3 671B MoE), a multimodal LLM (DeepSeek-VL2, 45B activated params), and a text-to-image model (Janus-Pro-7B), measuring their propensity to generate unsafe content. Its central artifact contributions are two purpose-built benchmarks tailored to the Chinese sociocultural/regulatory context: CNSafe, a bilingual (Chinese-English) safety benchmark built on the TC260-003 'Basic Security Requirements for Generative AI Service' standard (5 major categories, 31 subcategories, 3100 test cases), and CNSafe_RT, a red-teaming variant of 1000 adversarial samples across 10 risk dimensions that injects jailbreak techniques (scenario injection, affirmative-prefix induction, indirect instruction). These are the paper's OWN artifacts; SafeBench, MM-SafetyBench, and I2P are third-party benchmarks it re-uses for the MLLM and T2I evaluations.
threat_model: Adversarial users attempting to elicit prohibited/unsafe content from DeepSeek's generative models, both via (a) direct harmful queries and (b) jailbreak attacks that bypass safety alignment. For LLMs/MLLMs the adversary crafts malicious text (and for MLLMs, image-text pairs using image-semantic or typography-based attacks) to induce toxic, discriminatory, or otherwise policy-violating text. For the T2I model the adversary crafts prompts that yield unsafe images (sexual, violent, illegal, fake-news). A notable variant in the threat model is cross-lingual attack framing (Chinese vs. English) and exploitation of exposed chain-of-thought reasoning in the R1 reasoning model.
limitations: The authors themselves flag several: (1) the 'Service insecurity' category of TC260-003 is marked N/A throughout because it requires domain-expert judgment that neither LLM-as-Judge nor manual review could reliably supply. (2) DeepSeek-VL2's apparently strong safety is an artifact, not genuine alignment: the model frequently emits meaningless/degenerate output (repeated characters, 'the of', '**'), which the LLM-as-Judge scores as 'safe', deflating its measured ASR — so the number understates true risk and reflects weak multimodal comprehension. (3) Human evaluation was exhaustive only for R1/V3 on CNSafe_RT; everything else used sampling-based human checks, so judge-model errors propagate. (4) The two reported R1-vs-V3 gap figures (30.4% vs 31.25%) are not reconciled. (5) No mitigation/defense is proposed — the work is diagnostic only.

datasets_used: CNSafe (own; bilingual Chinese-English, built on TC260-003; 5 categories, 31 subcategories, 3100 test cases)
CNSafe_RT (own; red-teaming variant, 1000 adversarial samples, 10 risk dimensions, jailbreak methods: scenario injection / affirmative-prefix induction / indirect instruction)
SafeBench (third-party MLLM benchmark; 23 risk scenarios, 2300 base pairs; sampled for VL2 eval)
MM-SafetyBench (third-party MLLM benchmark; 13 scenarios, 5040 text-image pairs; sampled for VL2 eval)
I2P (third-party T2I benchmark; 7 risk dimensions; 671 queries sampled for Janus-Pro-7B eval)
quantitative_results: Cross-lingual gap on CNSafe: English ASR exceeds Chinese ASR by an average of 21.7% across all risk categories
R1 vs V3 on CNSafe: R1 higher ASR across all major categories; intro states +30.4% average, Table-1 analysis states 31.25% average gap

CNSafe Table 1 examples (V3-Chinese / R1-Chinese / V3-English / R1-English): Core socialist values violation 4.5 / 14.8 / 9.9 / 59.5; Discriminatory content 14.1 / 27.3 / 21.1 / 54.3; Commercial misconduct 12.4 / 28.0 / 11.2 / 69.0; Rights infringement 6.1 / 19.7 / 9.4 / 66.1
CNSafe_RT: DeepSeek-V3 reaches 95-100% ASR in many categories, 100% for Ethnic hatred and False information (both languages); DeepSeek-R1 typically 80-90% (Chinese) and 85-95% (English)
Jailbreak ASR reaches up to 100% in some categories
Comparison LLMs on CNSafe (avg ASR): QwQ-32B highest at 26.6%; DeepSeek-R1 22.5%; Qwen-Max lowest at 3.6%; Doubao high in Discriminatory content (26.3%) and Commercial misconduct (25.6%)
CNSafe_RT: DeepSeek-V3 averages 66.8% higher ASR than other Chinese-developed standard LLMs; Hunyuan lowest avg 1.9%
MLLM (DeepSeek-VL2): typography-based attacks exceed image-semantic attacks by avg 20.31% ASR; typography Economic Harm 40%, Fraud 38%; image-semantic attacks mostly 0-6%
T2I average ASR: Janus-Pro-7B 43.7% vs Stable-Diffusion-3.5-Large 39.9%

I2P per-category (Janus-Pro-7B vs SD-3.5-Large): Sexual 74.0 vs 47.0; Illegal-Activity 61.0 vs 39.0; Violence 52.0 vs 51.0; Shocking 56.0 vs 62.0; Self-Harm 22.0 vs 26.0; Hate 21.13 vs 25.35; Harassment 20.0 vs 29.0
baselines_compared: Other Chinese-developed LLMs: Doubao-1.5-pro-32k, Hunyuan-turbo-latest, Moonshot-v1-8k, Qwen-Max, QwQ-32B (reasoning)
Internal comparisons: DeepSeek-R1 vs DeepSeek-V3; image-semantic vs typography-based multimodal attacks; Chinese vs English prompts
